request.question: In what year was Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School's 1966 magazine project collected and published in book form?

title: When the Twerms Came
Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School
Foxfire (magazine)
Andrew Jackson Ritchie
Roald Dahl: Collected Stories
Alessandro Ludovico
The Headmaster (short story)
Sanshirō (novel)
The Queen of Zamba
Gnome Press
sentences: "When the Twerms Came" is a short story by British author Arthur C. Clarke published in the May 1972 edition of "Playboy" magazine.

It deals with an invasion of Earth one wet Tuesday afternoon by deadly Twerms and was published in book form in "The View from Serendip" and "More Than One Universe" collections, but was not included in the "The Collected Stories of Arthur C. Clarke".
Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School (informally known as Rabun Gap) is a small, private college preparatory school located in Rabun County, Georgia, United States, in the Appalachian Mountains.
It is both a boarding and a day school.

Rabun Gap is notable for initiating the "Foxfire" magazine project in 1966, experiential education based on interviewing local people, and writing and publishing articles about their stories and oral traditions.
This inspired numerous schools across the country to develop similar programs.

The Foxfire magazine began in 1966, written and published as a quarterly American magazine by students at Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School, a private secondary education school located in the U.S. state of Georgia.
At the time Foxfire began, Rabun Gap Nacoochee School was also operating as a public secondary education school for students who were residents of northern Rabun County, Georgia.
An example of experiential education, the magazine had articles based on the students' interviews with local people about aspects and practices in Appalachian culture.
They captured oral history, craft traditions, and other material about the culture.
When the articles were collected and published in book form in 1972, it became a bestseller nationally and gained attention for the Foxfire project.
Andrew Jackson Ritchie (January 30, 1868–1948) was the founder of Rabun Gap Industrial School, which later merged with Nacoochee Institute to become Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School.
He served there as president until 1939.
Roald Dahl: Collected Stories is a hardcover edition of short-stories by Roald Dahl for adults.
It was published in the US in October 2006 by Random House as part of the Everyman Library.
The present volume includes for the first time all the stories in chronological order as established by Dahl's biographer, Jeremy Treglown, in consultation with the Dahl estate.
A few of the short stories were not published chronologically in book form, but appeared later, collected in "More Tales of the Unexpected" (1980).
The collection contains all of the short stories published in the following collections:
Alessandro Ludovico is a researcher, artist and chief editor of Neural magazine since 1993.
He received his Ph.D. degree in English and Media from Anglia Ruskin University in Cambridge (UK).
He is Associate Professor at the Winchester School of Art, University of Southampton and Lecturer at Parsons Paris - The New School.
He has published and edited several books, and has lectured worldwide.
He also served as an advisor for the Documenta 12's Magazine Project.
He is one of the authors of the award-winning Hacking Monopolism trilogy of artworks (Google Will Eat Itself), Amazon Noir), Face to Facebook).
The Headmaster is a short story by the British mystery and thriller writer Michael Gilbert about the counterspies Mr. Calder and Mr. Behrens.

First published in the United Kingdom in the June, 1962, issue of "Argosy", it was later published in the United States in "Ellery Queen's Mystery Magazine" and then collected in book form along with other stories about the same two protagonists as the sixth of eleven stories in "Game without Rules".

It is set in a contemporary, but undated London, and, unlike most of the other stories in this series, has Mr. Calder as the main character, with only brief appearances by Mr. Behrens and Rasselas, the Persian deerhound.

It is written in Gilbert's usual spare style, but with even fewer overtones and descriptions of the characters than are found in most of his works; the plot itself, and its resolution, is also somewhat unlikely, so the story, compared to the others in the series, must be considered a fairly minor one.
It does, however, have several examples of Gilbert's understated and astringent humor.
Sanshirō (三四郎 ) is a full-length novel by the Japanese writer Natsume Sōseki.
The novel was originally published as a serialized work in the Japanese newspaper Asahi Shimbun from September 1 through December 29 of 1908.
The Shunyōdō Shoten Company published it in book form in May 1909.
The book form is divided into 13 chapters.
Sanshirō is the first in a trilogy of thematically related novels, along with subsequent works And Then and The Gate.
The Queen of Zamba is a science fiction novel written by L. Sprague de Camp, the first book of his "Viagens Interplanetarias" series and its subseries of stories set on the fictional planet Krishna.
It was written between November 1948 and January 1949 and first published in the magazine "Astounding Science Fiction" as a two-part serial in the issues for August and September 1949.
It was first published in book form as a paperback by Ace Books in 1954 as an "Ace Double" issued back-to-back with Clifford D. Simak's novel "Ring Around the Sun".
This version was editorially retitled Cosmic Manhunt and introduced a number of textual changes disapproved by the author.
The novel was first issued by itself in another paperback edition under the title A Planet Called Krishna, published in England by Compact Books in 1966.
A new paperback edition restoring the author's preferred title and text and including the Krishna short story "Perpetual Motion" was published by Dale Books in 1977.
This edition was reprinted by Ace Books in 1982 as part of the standard edition of the Krishna novels.
The novel has been translated into German, French, Italian, Czech, and Polish.
An E-book edition was published by Gollancz's SF Gateway imprint on September 29, 2011 as part of a general release of de Camp's works in electronic form.
Gnome Press was an American small-press publishing company primarily known for publishing many science fiction classics.
Gnome was one of the most eminent of the fan publishers of SF, producing 86 titles in its lifespan — many considered classic works of SF and Fantasy today.

Gnome was important in the transitional period between Genre SF as a magazine phenomenon and its arrival in mass-market book publishing, but proved too underfunded to make the leap from fan-based publishing to the professional level.
The company existed for just over a decade, ultimately failing due to inability to compete with major publishers who also started to publish science fiction.

In its heyday, Gnome published many of the major SF authors, and in some cases, as with Robert E Howard's Conan series (published in six books from 1950 – 1955) and Isaac Asimov's Foundation series (published in three books from 1951 – 1953), was responsible for the manner in which their stories were collected into book form.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: In what year was Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School's 1966 magazine project collected and published in book form?
hop1_docs: Foxfire (magazine) | The Foxfire magazine began in 1966, written and published as a quarterly American magazine by students at Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School, a private secondary education school located in the U.S. state of Georgia.  At the time Foxfire began, Rabun Gap Nacoochee School was also operating as a public secondary education school for students who were residents of northern Rabun County, Georgia.  An example of experiential education, the magazine had articles based on the students' interviews with local people about aspects and practices in Appalachian culture.  They captured oral history, craft traditions, and other material about the culture.  When the articles were collected and published in book form in 1972, it became a bestseller nationally and gained attention for the Foxfire project.

Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School | Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School (informally known as Rabun Gap) is a small, private college preparatory school located in Rabun County, Georgia, United States, in the Appalachian Mountains.  It is both a boarding and a day school.  Rabun Gap is notable for initiating the "Foxfire" magazine project in 1966, experiential education based on interviewing local people, and writing and publishing articles about their stories and oral traditions.  This inspired numerous schools across the country to develop similar programs.

Andrew Jackson Ritchie | Andrew Jackson Ritchie (January 30, 1868–1948) was the founder of Rabun Gap Industrial School, which later merged with Nacoochee Institute to become Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School.  He served there as president until 1939.

Rabun County High School | Rabun County High School is a public high school operated by the Rabun County School District.  It is located on the edge of Tiger, a town in Rabun County in the U.S. state of Georgia.  It is the original venue of "Foxfire" magazine and related projects.  Notable alumni include current head football coach Lee Shaw, father of Chicago Bears quarterback Connor Shaw and current University of Georgia

What If the Moon Didn't Exist | What if the Moon Didn’t Exist is a collection of speculative articles about different versions of Earth, published in book form in 1993.  They were originally published in "Astronomy" magazine.  The individual scenarios are:

Eliot Wigginton | Eliot Wigginton (born Brooks Eliot Wigginton on November 9, 1942) is an American oral historian, folklorist, writer and former educator.  He was most widely known for developing the Foxfire Project, a writing project that led to a magazine and the series of best-selling "Foxfire" books, twelve volumes in all.  These were based on articles by high school students from Rabun County, Georgia.  In 1986 he was named "Georgia Teacher of the Year" and in 1989 he was awarded a MacArthur Fellowship.

The Productions of Time | The Productions of Time is a science fiction novel written by John Brunner and first published in "The Magazine of Fantasy and Science Fiction" in 1966.  It appeared in book form the following year, published by Signet Books.
hop1_titles: What If the Moon Didn't Exist
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- "Foxfire (magazine)" — [1] "began in 1966"
- "Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School" — [1] "Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School"; [2] "Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School (informally known as Rabun Gap)"
- Book publication year — [1] "When the articles were collected and published in book form in 1972"
- "Rabun County High School" — [4] "original venue of \"Foxfire\" magazine"
- "Eliot Wigginton" — [6] "developing the Foxfire Project"

Ambiguities:
- Origin/venue conflict: [1] ties Foxfire to "Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School" while [4] calls "Rabun County High School" the "original venue of \"Foxfire\" magazine".
- "Foxfire" label variants: passages refer to "Foxfire (magazine)", "Foxfire Project", and "Foxfire" books (possible multiple senses).

Bridge / missing fact:
- The specific relation asked (year the 1966 magazine project was collected and published in book form) is explicitly present and unambiguous: "1972" in [1]. No additional bridge needed for the year.

Retrieval direction:
- Open page "Foxfire (magazine)" to confirm publication history and 1972 collection.
- Open page "Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School" for institutional context of the 1966 project.
- (Optional) Open "Rabun County High School" to resolve venue/origin discrepancy.
Final answer (from provided passages): 1972.
